coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of hemicellulose/deep eutectic solvent based carbon quantum dots for ultrasensitive detection of Ag+ and L-cysteine with “off-on” pattern
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Xueqin
Huang, Jianbo
Chen, Tianying
Zhao, Qiang
Xu, Feng
Zhang, Xueming
coredata.dc:description: The hypersensitive detection of metal ions and amino acid becomes more important for environmental and human physical conditions monitor but remains still a great challenge. In this work, we have reported a green and straight forward procedure (solvothermal treatment) for the synthesis of N-doped hemicellulose-based carbon quantum dots (N-H-CQDs) used as an “off-on” fluorescence sensor for ultrasensitive detecting Ag+ ion and L-Cysteine (L-Cys). During this process, hemicellulose used as carbon source and nitrogen-rich deep eutectic solvent (DES) of choline chloride/urea acted as both solvent and doping agent. The prepared N-H-CQDs displayed narrow diameter ranging from 2 to 8 nm with relatively higher quantum yield (QY) of 23.45%. This probe was able to selectively detect Ag+ ion on account of the formation of N-H-CQDs- Ag+ complex and possessed extremely low detection limit of 21 nM. Additionally, the fluorescence of N-H-CQDs-Ag+ complex was restored by the addition of L-Cysteine as Ag+ ion would assemble a more stable Ag+-L-Cysteine complex through Ag+-thiol bonds. Accordingly, the N-H-CQDs-Ag+ solution behaved as secondary fluorescent probe to detect L-Cys in the dynamic range of 0–100 μM with a LOD of 242 nM. The prepared N-H-CQDs are promising probes for hypersensitive detection of Ag+ and L-Cys.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hemicellulose
Carbon quantum dots
DES
Silver ion
L-Cysteine

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of choline chloride/urea based DES and dissolution of hemicellulose
Synthesis of N-H-CQDs via solvothermal treatment
Fluorescence stability at different pH values and time
Synthesis of N-H-CQDs in DES via solvothermal treatment
Optical properties and fluorescence stability of N-H-CQDs
Detection of Ag+ by fluorescence quenching

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence emission spectra of N-H-CQDs with variations of excitation wavelength. Solvothermal treatment of (a) 5 h, (b) 8 h, (c) 12 h.
(a) TEM photograph of N-H-CQDs (inset: HR-TEM photograph of N-H-CQDs). (b) AFM image of N-H-CQDs. (c) XRD spectrum of N-H-CQDs. (d) FTIR spectra of hemicellulose and N-H-CQDs.
XPS survey spectrum of (a) hemicellulose and (b) N-H-CQDs. High resolution XPS spectra of (c) C 1s, (d) N 1 s of N-H-CQDs.

(a) UV–Vis and photoluminescence spectra of N-H-CQDs. Inset: N-H-CQDs aqueous solution under sunlight (left) and irradiation with 360 nm UV light (right). PL intensity of N-H-CQDs solutions at different pH values (b) and time (c). (d) PL lifetime curves of N-H-CQDs.

(a) The fluorescent images of N-H-CQDs solutions without (left) and with presence of Ag+ ion under irradiation with UV light. (b) The PL emission spectra response of N-H-CQDs with increasing of Ag+ concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, and 200 μM). (c) Relative PL intensity of N-H-CQDs in the function of Ag+ concentrations. (d) The linear relationship between F0/F and the concentrations of Ag+ ranging from 2.5 μM to 100 μM. (e) The fluorescent response of N-H-CQDs with increasing of Ag+ concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1and 2.5 μM). (f) The linear relationship between F0/F and the concentrations of Ag+ ranging from 0 μM to 2.5 μM.
Selectivity of N-H-CQDs for Ag+. F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of N-H-CQDs with and without metal ions. All competing metal ions were 200 μM.

(a) The fluorescent emission spectra of N-H-CQDs and the fluorescent response of N-H-CQDs-Ag+ solution with increasing of L-Cys concentrations (from bottom to top: 0, 1, 2.5, 10, 25, 50 and 100 μM). (b)The linear relationship between FR/F and the concentrations of L-Cys ranging from 0 to100 μM.
Schematic of the preparation of N-H-CQDs and its detection for Ag+ and L-Cysteine.
The performance of various fluorescent quantum dots for Ag+ ion detection.
Detection of Ag+ using N-H-CQDs in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of hemicellulose/deep eutectic solvent based carbon quantum dots for ultrasensitive detection of Ag+ and L-cysteine with “off-on” pattern

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing, PR China, 100083

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing, P. R. China, 100083

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The hypersensitive detection of metal ions and amino acid becomes more important for environmental and human physical conditions monitor but remains still a great challenge. In this work, we have reported a green and straight forward procedure (solvothermal treatment) for the synthesis of N-doped hemicellulose-based carbon quantum dots (N-H-CQDs) used as an “off-on” fluorescence sensor for ultrasensitive detecting Ag+ ion and L-Cysteine (L-Cys). During this process, hemicellulose used as carbon source and nitrogen-rich deep eutectic solvent (DES) of choline chloride/urea acted as both solvent and doping agent. The prepared N-H-CQDs displayed narrow diameter ranging from 2 to 8 nm with relatively higher quantum yield (QY) of 23.45%. This probe was able to selectively detect Ag+ ion on account of the formation of N-H-CQDs- Ag+ complex and possessed extremely low detection limit of 21 nM. Additionally, the fluorescence of N-H-CQDs-Ag+ complex was restored by the addition of L-Cysteine as Ag+ ion would assemble a more stable Ag+-L-Cysteine complex through Ag+-thiol bonds. Accordingly, the N-H-CQDs-Ag+ solution behaved as secondary fluorescent probe to detect L-Cys in the dynamic range of 0–100 μM with a LOD of 242 nM. The prepared N-H-CQDs are promising probes for hypersensitive detection of Ag+ and L-Cys.

ce:section.ce:para: The heavy metals pollution is getting worse due to the emissions of pollutants in modern industry [1]. As an important kind of heavy metal, Ag+ ion has been extensively applied in the photographic industry, decoration, electronic and other fields, resulting in widely spreading in human daily life owing to the attractive performance and wide range of applications [2]. However, Ag+ ion is easier to bond with enzymes/proteins, leading to the inactivation of enzymes/proteins [3]. Therefore, highly sensitive detection of Ag+ ion becomes extraordinary important for the human health monitoring and environmental protection. In addition, L-Cysteine (L-Cys) plays a significant function in keeping physiological redox homeostasis [4]. Disordered levels of L-Cys can lead to some serious problems for human health, including cystinosis, cardiovascular disease, liver damage, skin lesion, and cancer [4–6]. Therefore, a simple, rapid, inexpensive probe for detecting Ag+ and L-Cys with the characteristic of lower detection limit is highly desirable.

So far, a number of methodologies for detecting Ag+ and L-Cys, including capillary electrophoresis, absorption spectrometry, and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) have been developed [7–11]. However, most methods for detecting Ag+ ion demand expensive instruments, complicated pretreatment procedures of samples. Therefore, developing more sensitive and simple approaches for Ag+ and L-Cys detections are urgently needed. Recently, CQDs have been applied in detection of metal ions and biomolecules because of highly sensitivity, selectivity and simple operation [12–14]. Among these metal ions, considerable interests have been directed into detecting silver ion due to their widely industrial applications and toxicity to organisms. Sun et al. has reported synthesis of carbon nanoparticles as an efficient fluorescent sensor to Ag+ with a detection limit of 500 pM [15]. In addition, Kim et al. has developed a simple and green strategy through hydrothermal treatment to synthesize water-soluble CQDs from broccoli. In this study, CQDs possessed a LOD of 0.5 μm and Ag+ was selectively detected due to the fluorescence quenching of CQDs [16].

Apart from quenching fluorescence of CQDs by addition of Ag+, the fluorescence enhancement effect of CQDs has also been reported for silver ion detection according to a metal-enhanced fluorescence mechanism [17]. It has been revealed that the reduced Ag nanoclusters by the CQDs can improve the local incident field of the CQDs, resulting in the improvement of fluorescence. Recently, a novel bifunctional fluorescent sensor has been prepared by Zhu et al. with the function of detection and removal of Ag+ in aqueous solutions [18]. This sensor was prepared through grafting naphthalimide-based probe on the silica nanoparticles, which exhibited remarkable enhancement of fluorescence due to the addition of silver ion (detection limit of 7.2 μm). Remarkably, L-Cysteine exhibited strong binding ability for metal ions and formed C-metal ion-C (CL-Cysteine) base pairs, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence [19]. In addition, synthesis of CQDs used for continuous detection of Hg2+ and L-Cysteine with an “off-on” pattern has also been studied [20,21]. Although some progressive results were achieved for synthesis of CQDs and detection of silver ion and L-Cysteine, synthesis of CQDs with high fluorescence quantum yield, more sensitive detection limits are still great challenge. Therefore, synthesis of CQDs with diverse composition and alterable structure such as stronger fluorescent properties with high quantum yield and detection limits, excellent chemical stability, and utilization of green and sustainable starting materials is still desirable.

As one of the most abundant natural resources, hemicellulose exhibits excellent properties, like biodegradability, nontoxicity, and good biocompatibility [22]. To date, only a few works on synthesis of hemicellulose-derived CQDs and use for bioimaging and sensor have been reported. Wang et al. have successfully synthesized CQDs from hemicelluloses using dilute NH4OH/waters as solvent via one-step hydrothermal carbonization [23]. Recently, Yang et al. have synthesized N-doped CQDs by dissolving xylan in ammonia solution through microwave-assisted method [24]. The obtained N-CQDs serve as sensors for detecting of tetracycline with a limit of 6.49 nm. Therefore, study on synthesis of hemicellulose-based CQDs with high sensitivity and selectivity used for detecting metal ions and other substances can provide a promising approach for the high value utilization of biomass resources.

Herein, we presented a green and simple approach to prepare N-doped hemicellulose carbon quantum dots (N-H-CQDs) used for selective and continuous detection of Ag+ and L-Cys. It has been reported that N-doped CQDs display higher fluorescence quantum yields, excellent electrochemical and optical properties [24,25]. The commonly used method for synthesis N-CQD is the use of nitrogen contained precursor, and the other is through chemical modification method for doping nitrogen [23–25]. Motivated by hemicellulose separation using deep eutectic solvent (DES), a class of novel solvent with non-toxicity, biodegradability, low cost and low vapor emission [26], a DES consisting of choline chloride/urea rich in nitrogen element was used as both solvent and nitrogen source for preparing N-doped hemicellulose carbon quantum dots through simple solvothermal treatment. The prepared N-H-CQDs displayed superior fluorescence, pH and optical stability and high quantum yield (QY, 23.45%). Additionally, N-H-CQDs exhibited outstanding sensitivity and selectivity for Ag+, with a LOD of 21 nM. Interestingly, the complex of Ag+ and N-H-CQDs could also serve as secondary sensors for detecting of L-Cys with a LOD of 242 nM.

Industrial hemicelluloses were kindly supplied by Fulida Fiber Corporation (Xinjiang province, China). These hemicellulose samples were obtained by alkali extraction using NaOH, neutralization and drying from production of dissolving pulp. The hemicellulosic samples were mainly consisted of xylose (67.07%) as well as with a small amount of mannose (6.38%) and glucose (2.51%). Choline chloride, L-Cysteine and quinine sulfate dehydrate were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide were supplied by Beijing Chemical Works. The obtained chemicals were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of choline chloride/urea based DES and dissolution of hemicellulose
ce:section.ce:para: Choline chloride (16 g) and urea (13.751 g) were heated at 60 °C with magnetic stirring in a 250 mL beaker for 10 min. After forming a clear colorless liquid, industrial hemicelluloses (1.78 g) were gradually added to this DES and reached the concentration of 6%. The mixture was stirred at 100 °C for 3 h, which finally formed a brownish yellow solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-H-CQDs via solvothermal treatment
ce:section.ce:para: The brown-yellow solution was transferred into Teflon-line autoclaves, which were then heated in a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 5, 8 and 12 h, respectively. After the thermal treatment and the muffle furnace was cooled down to 30 °C, the mixture was washed with distilled water and filtrated. Then the supernatant was dialyzed for 3 days using dialysis membranes with retained molecular weight of 1000 Da. Finally, the obtained bright yellow solution was concentrated by evaporation at 60 °C and freeze-dried. After that, the N-H-CQDs powders were obtained and dispersed in distilled water (0.05 mg/mL) for further use.

TEM and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were taken in a Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN instrument. AFM photograph was obtained at a Bruker MultiMode 8 AFM. XRD pattern of N-H-CQDs was measured with a Bruker AXS D8 with Cu radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) at 40 kV and 40 mA. The FTIR spectrum was recorded on a Nicolet iN10 FTIR by using KBr pellets within the range of 500-4000 cm−1. XPS survey spectra were performed with an ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer using Al Kα source. The UV–Vis spectrum was carried out on a UV2310II spectrometer. The PL spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrofluorometer. The PL decay curve was measured on a steady state and transient state fluorescence spectrometer excited at 360 nm.

The QYs of N-H-CQDs were calculated by choosing quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent) as the reference standard (QY is 54%). The PL spectra of quinine sulfate and N-H-CQDs were measured at maximum excitation wavelengths of 360 nm and the QYs were obtained according to the following equation:
Q n
=
Q st
I n
I st
×
A st
A n
×
η n 2
η st 2
× 100 %

Here, Q is the QY. I and A refer to the area of fluorescence peak and UV–Vis absorbance, respectively. η stands for the refractive index of solvent. st and n are quinine sulfate and N-H-CQDs, respectively, The UV–Vis absorbance of quinine sulfate and as-prepared N-H-CQDs solutions at maximum excitation wavelength were adjusted to less than 0.1 to prevent reabsorption by both quinine sulfate and as-prepared N-H-CQDs solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence stability at different pH values and time
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared N-H-CQDs aqueous solutions were adjusted to different pH values ranging from 1 to 11 by adding 1 M NaOH and 1 M HCl. After 10 min, the corresponding PL spectra of N-H-CQDs were measured. Besides, the N-H-CQDs solutions were placed at room temperature and the fluorescence spectra were measured every 10 days.

The principle for detection of Ag+ was carried out according to the fluorescence quenching. In general, the prepared N-H-CQDs (2 mL) was added in Ag+ aqueous solution (2 mL) with different concentrations (0, 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, and 200 μM). After retention for 10 min, the fluorescence spectra of N-H-CQDs mixture solutions were recorded under excitation of 360 nm. In order to demonstrate the selectivity of N-H-CQDs toward Ag+, various metal ions, such as Co2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Pb2+ and Mn2+ were added into N-H-CQDs aqueous solution (2 mL) at a concentration of 200 μM. Then the fluorescence intensity of N-H-CQDs and metal ions mixture solutions were recorded after 10 min retention.

In addition, the water sample from Qinghe River in Beijing was used to evaluate the practicality of N-H-CQDs as sensors for Ag+ detection. The experiments of N-H-CQDs aqueous solution for the detection of Ag+ was repeated by using river water instead of deionized water to prepare Ag+ solution with different concentrations (0.05, 0.1, 1, and 25 μM). The river water was collected from Qinghe River in Beijing, China, filtered before use.

The detection of L-Cys using N-H-CQDs-Ag+ complexes was carried out based on “turn-on” fluorescence due to the dissociation of N-H-CQDs-Ag+ complexes. In general, 2 mL aqueous solution of L-Cys with different concentrations (0, 5, 10, 20, 50 and 100 μM) were added in 2 mL N-H-CQDs-Ag+ complexes solution (Ag+ concentration 200 μM), respectively. The fluorescence spectra excited at 360 nm were recorded when the mixture solutions were equilibrated for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-H-CQDs in DES via solvothermal treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Hemicelluloses, as the second abundant carbohydrates, are consisted of pentoses, hexoses, and glucuronic acids with a linear main chain and a variety of short branched chains, which exhibit great potential in various applications such as chemical industries, pharmaceutical industries, and tissue engineering due to their biocompatibility, biodegradability, sustainability and renewability [27]. In the present work, nitrogen doped hemicellulose-based CQDs (N-H-CQDs) were synthesized using DES as both solvent and nitrogen source for selective detection of silver ion and L-Cys. The main mechanism for the synthesis of N-H-CQDs and detection of silver ion and L-Cys is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Aiming at enhancing the fluorescent properties of N-H-CQDs, the nitrogen-rich DES composed of choline chloride and urea was chosen as solvent, in which choline chloride/urea solution was formed through the hydrogen bond interactions of self-association and choline chloride and urea acted as hydrogen bond acceptor and hydrogen bond donors, respectively [28]. Remarkable, the DES of choline chloride/urea solution exhibited outstanding dissolving hemicellulose abilities due to the disruption of the intermolecular hydrogen bonding of polysaccharides [29]. After solvothermal treatment, the N-H-CQDs were prepared after four stages, which was similar with studies on the hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment of biomass [30,31]. Amazingly, the prepared N-H-CQDs possessed abundant nitrogen and oxygen contained groups, which might promote the fluorescence properties of the N-H-CQDs [32]. The N-H-CQDs exhibited higher sensitivity toward detection of Ag+ due to formation of complexes between surface groups in CQDs and silver ion, resulting in fluorescence quenching [33,34]. Notably, the fluorescence was renewed with the introduction of L-Cys as it displayed stronger complexation ability for Ag+ than N-H-CQDs. The similar results were also observed in detection of Hg2+ and L-cysteine of carbon dots obtained from pineapple peel with fluorescence “off-on” pattern [35]. Therefore, the prepared N-H-CQDs exhibited great potential for sensitive detection of silver ion and L-Cys with “off-on” pattern.

The effect of treatment time on N-H-CQDs PL spectra was firstly studied, in which hemicellulose were treated in DES for 5, 8 and 12 h, respectively. The fluorescence emissions spectra excited at different wavelengths were recorded (Fig.1 ). As the PL spectra of N-H-CQDs were excited in the range of 300–460 nm, the emission peak generally shifted to a higher wavelength, which might be ascribed to the “edge red-shit effect” [36]. It was also noted that the maximum excitation wavelengths of N-H-CQDs synthesized via solvothermal for 5 h, 8 h and 12 h could be assigned to 340 nm, 360 nm and 360 nm, respectively. Additionally, the fluorescence quantum yields of N-H-CQDs prepared by this treatment for 5 h, 8 h and 12 h were 21.84%, 23.45%, and 25.68% with quinine sulfate as a reference, respectively. Considering fluorescence stability, quantum yield and economic efficiency, the N-H-CQDs treated for 8 h was used for further analysis and characterizations.

The TEM and AFM photographs of prepared N-H-CQDs by solvothermal treatment at 200 °C for 8 h are presented in Figs.2 . (a) and (b). The TEM and AFM images indicated that the N-H-CQDs were quasi-spherical without obvious agglomeration, indicating that N-H-CQDs were uniformly dispersed in an aqueous solution with good water solubility. The particle size of N-H-CQDs was mainly 2–8 nm, and the average diameters size was calculated to be 5.4 nm. Besides, the lattice morphology of N-H-CQD could be clearly observed from the HR-TEM image with a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, similar to the (100) plane of graphite [37].

The XRD pattern of N-H-CQDs (Fig. 2c) exhibited broad peak at 2θ = 24.57°, which was attributed to the (002) facet of graphite [38]. The FTIR of hemicellulose and N-H-CQDs were further analyzed as revealed in Fig. 2(d). The characteristic peaks of N-H-CQD were similar to that of hemicellulose, in which the peaks at 1160 and 890 cm−1 were the characteristics peaks of xylan [39]. The peak of 890 cm−1 was attributed to the strong vibration absorption peak of β-(1–4) glycosidic bonds between sugar units [39]. The absorption peak at 1045 cm−1 belonged to stretching vibration of COC. The peaks at 3600-3200 cm−1were related with the vibration of OH and NH. However, the intensity of N-H-CQDs absorption peaks at 1160–890 cm−1 was significantly decreased compared with that of hemicellulose, which might be caused by the degradation of hemicellulose during the solvothermal process. Besides, the stretching vibration of NH and CC peaks at 1581–1720 cm−1 were obviously enhanced. Moreover, a strong peak at 1405 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of CN could be observed clearly. The formation of NH bonds and CN bonds demonstrated that nitrogen element was successfully incorporated into the hemicellulose-based CQDs as treated with choline chloride/urea, resulting in preparation of nitrogen-doped CQDs.

The elemental composition and chemical bonds of hemicellulose and N-H-CQDs were analyzed with XPS (Fig. 3 ). The survey spectrum of hemicellulose (Fig. 3a) showed two distinct peaks at 533.eV and 285 eV, which were assigned to O1s and C1s, respectively. Besides, the atomic percentages of C and O elements were 78.83% and 21.17%, respectively. The survey spectrum of N-H-CQDs in Fig. 3(b) exhibited three distinct peaks at 533.eV, 399 eV and 285 eV (ascribed to O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively), demonstrating that hemicellulose-based nitrogen doped CQDs were successfully prepared, which was in agreement with the FTIR analysis. Furthermore, the atomic percentages of C, N, and O in the prepared N-H-CQDs were 83.22%, 6.55%, and 10.23%, respectively. It was noted from Fig. 3(c) that the high-resolution C1s spectrum of N-H-CQDs was divided into mainly four peaks at 284.2, 285.1, 286 and 288.3 eV, which was attributed to CC/CO, CN, CO and CO/CN bonds, respectively [40,41]. For the high-resolution N1s spectrum of N-H-CQDs (Fig. 3d), an apparent peak at 399.1 eV attributed to the NH2 groups was observed. In summary, based on the results in XPS characterization and FTIR, it was demonstrated that the nitrogen-doped CQDs with multiple functional groups such as hydroxyl, amino and carbonyl were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and fluorescence stability of N-H-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis spectra and photoluminescence spectra of N-H-CQDs are depicted in Fig. 4 . The N-H-CQDs aqueous solution exhibited yellow transparent liquid (left) under sunlight, while the aqueous solution showed bright blue fluorescence (right) under irradiation with 360 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 4a). In Fig. 4(a), the N-H-CQDs displayed a distinct peak at 277 nm and a wide absorption band around at 360 nm. The absorption peak at 277 nm was attributed to the π-π* transitions of the aromatic CC in sp2 hybrid domains, indicating that CC was formed during the solvothermal process. Meanwhile, the peak at 360 nm belonged to the n → π* transition of C=O/C-OH bond in sp3 hybrid regions, leading to strong fluorescence caused by the trapping of excited state energy from the surface states of N-H-CQDs. Besides, as excited at 360 nm in Fig. 4(a), the N-H-CQDs exhibited the strongest PL emission at 436 nm with QY of 23.45% using quinine sulfate as a reference.

The pH stability and fluorescence durability of the prepared N-H-CQDs solutions are shown in Figs. 4 (b) and (c). As noted in Fig. 4 (b), the fluorescence intensity remained relatively stable when the pH varied from 3 to 9, whereas slightly decrease was observed under strong acidic or strong alkaline condition. The PL stability of N-H-CQDs solution was analyzed at different storage time (10, 20, 30 and 40 days, Fig. 4c). Obviously, the fluorescence intensity of N-H-CQDs reduced gradually as storage time increased, and around 85% of initial intensity was maintained after 30 days. In conclusion, the prepared N-H-CQDs possessed superior pH and time stability, which might be used in the monitoring environmental water samples under different pH conditions.

The fluorescence decay and biexponential fit curves for N-H-CQDs could be observed in Fig. 4(d). The decay curve was fitted according to the following formula:
Y
t
=
A 1
exp
− t /
τ 1
+
A 2
exp
− t /
τ 2

Here, τ1 and τ2 are decay lifetimes, t is time, A 1 and A2 are fractional intensities of τ1 and τ2, respectively. The parameters have been obtained from the instrument response function (IRF) like τ1 was 2.28 ns, τ2 was 6.49 ns and χ = 1.022, indicating that decay curve fitted very well. The average PL lifetime (τave) was calculated according to the following formula:
Τ ave
=
A 1
τ 1
2
+
A 2
τ 2
2
/
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2

The τave of N-H-CQDs was calculated to be 4.1 ns. Therefore, the N-H-CQD prepared in this work has a good degree of fit, and the average fluorescence lifetime is 2.12 time longer than that of N-CQDs prepared by Liang et al. [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ by fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence photographs of N-H-CQDs aqueous solution with and without Ag+ under irradiation with 360 nm UV light are shown in Fig. 5(a). The N-H-CQDs aqueous solution exhibited strong blue fluorescence (left) without Ag+ and fluorescence quenching occurred with the presence of Ag+ ion (right). Therefore, the feasibility and sensitivity of using N-H-CQDs toward detecting Ag+ at different concentrations (0, 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, and 200 μM) were investigated as shown in Fig. 5(b). As the concentrations of Ag+ increased from 0 to 200 μM, the PL intensity of N-H-CQDs excited at 360 nm was gradually decreased, indicating that the prepared N-H-CQDs could be employed for detecting of Ag+ ion. Besides, Fig. 5(c) depicted the change of relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) with the increase of Ag+ ion concentrations, where F and F0 are the PL intensities of N-H-CQDs solution with and without of Ag+ ions. As the Ag+ concentrations increased from 0 to 2.5 μM, the F/F0 values decreased dramatically, and while it decreased gradually when Ag+ concentrations increased from 2.5 to 100 μM. Fig. 5(d) showed a good linear relationship between F0/F and Ag+ concentrations from 2.5 to 100 μM. In addition, the fluorescence response of N-H-CQDs upon addition of Ag+ ion ranging from 0 to 2.5 μM was further studied as shown in Fig. 5(e).

The PL intensity of N-H-CQDs declined gradually with the increase of Ag+ ion concentrations from 0 to 2.5 μM. Additionally, a good linear relationship between (F0/F) and Ag+ concentrations was observed (Fig. 5f). The linear relationship was calculated by the Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
C

KSV is the quenching constant and C represents the concentration of Ag+. The KSV of the linear regression equation (R2 = 0.9910) over the range from 0 to 2.5 μM is 0.2286. Therefore, the detection limit of N-H-CQDs for Ag+ was calculated to be 21 nM. As observed in Table 1 , the comparative performances of CQDs for silver ion detection are summarized. Obviously, due to the relatively lower detection limits, the sensing behaviors of N-H-CQDs for Ag+ ion in this work were superior to that from other works. Therefore, the prepared N-H-CQDs could be applied as a fluorescent probe for detecting of Ag+ ion with a high sensitivity.

Some carbon dots have been used for detection of metal ions in real water samples, which exhibited superior results in detecting Hg2+ in lake and tap water [35]. So as to verify the real application of N-H-CQDs for the assay of Ag+, the detection of N-H-CQDs for Ag+ in river water was performed as shown in Table 2 . The recovery varied between 98.30% and 108.75% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 1.32, which demonstrated that the N-H-CQDs was relatively accurate and could be used to detect Ag+ in environmental water sample.

Apart from the sensitivity for Ag+ ion, the selectivity for detection of metal ions is also significant. In order to demonstrate the selective detection of N-H-CQDs toward Ag+, we tested the PL intensity of N-H-CQDs with various metal ions including Co2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Cu2+, and Mn2+ cations at a concentration of 200 μM (Fig.6 .). It was noted that Ag+ ion induced obvious fluorescence quenching for N-H-CQDs, and Cu2+ could also influence the PL intensity of N-H-CQDs. Additionally, the change of fluorescence intensity caused by other metal ions could be negligible, which demonstrated that the N-H-CQDs exhibited highly selective for detection of Ag+ ion.

The fluorescent change of N-H-CQDs-Ag+ solution for detection of L-Cys was evaluated and the results are shown in Fig.7 . As noted in Fig. 7(a), the PL intensity of N-H-CQDs/Ag+ solution gradually enhanced as the L-Cys concentrations increased, which suggested that the N-H-CQDs-Ag+ solution could be served as fluorescent probe for L-Cys.

The relative fluorescence intensity (FR/F) and L-Cys concentrations exhibited a linear relationship as the L-Cys ranged from 0 to 100 μM. FR/F are the PL intensity of N-H-CQDs-Ag+ solution with and without L-Cys. The linear relationship was calculated by the Stern-Volmer equation:
F R
F
= 1 +
K SV
C

KSV is the quenching constant and C represents the concentrations of L-Cys. The KSV of the linear regression equation with the coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.9922) is 0.01471 in the range of L-Cys from 0 to 100 μM. The LOD of N-H-CQDs-Ag+ toward L-Cys was 242 nM. The results demonstrated that the N-H-CQDs-Ag+ could be acted as sensing probe with “off-on” pattern toward the detection of L-Cys.

In conclusion, nitrogen-doped hemicellulose-based carbon quantum dots (N-H-CQDs) with high quantum yield and optical stability have been successfully prepared via simple solvothermal treatment. Here, hemicellulose used as the carbon source and DES of choline chloride/urea acted as both solvent and nitrogen source for the first time. Remarkable, the fluorescence of N-H-CQDs was quenched with the addition of Ag+ and quickly recovered with the presence of L-Cys. Therefore, the prepared N-H-CQDs could be severed as “off-on” fluorescent probes for the detection of Ag+ and L-Cys with lower detection limits of 21 nM and 242 nM, respectively. Besides, the N-H-CQDs exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Ag+ and was validated in real river water. Therefore, with low toxicity, inexpensive and wide source, hemicellulose/DES derived CQDs are promising in the fields of fluorescent sensors for the detection of silver ion and L-Cys.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the financial support of this research from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFD0600803) and Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31470606).
